question_title: Will the “Yes” side receive strictly more than 60.0% of valid votes in the 2026 Maldives constitutional referendum?
summary: The 2026 Maldivian constitutional referendum (April 4) asks whether to merge presidential and parliamentary elections. The ruling PNC has a 75/93-seat supermajority, the bill passed 72-73 in parliament, and the proposal is framed as a cost-saving efficiency measure. Multiple parties (PNC, MDA, JDP) support Yes. However, the main opposition MDP actively campaigns No, framing it as a power grab, and former presidents Nasheed and Solih oppose. No independent polls exist. I modeled Yes vote share as Beta distribution centered at ~65% with 8% std dev. P(Yes > 60%) ≈ 70% from simulation, discounted slightly for cancellation risk to ~67%.

factors.description: PNC supermajority (75/93 seats) and 2024 landslide (66 seats alone) demonstrates massive ruling party support among voters
Parliamentary vote was 72/73 in favor - near-unanimous among those who voted, showing cross-party legislative support
Proposal is framed as cost-saving efficiency measure (combining elections), which polls well universally. Government claims 90% survey support (biased source but directionally informative)
Government controls state media, institutional advantages, and is running combined with local elections where PNC will mobilize voters
2007 Maldives referendum precedent: presidential system won 'over 60%' when government backed it, showing government-backed referendums can clear 60% threshold
Opposition MDP actively campaigning No, former presidents Nasheed and Solih opposing, framing as 'constitutional coup' and power grab
Referendums can surprise - voters sometimes reject government proposals even with strong parliamentary support
Economic stress (fuel crisis, debt, IMF warnings) may reduce government credibility and hurt Yes vote
No independent polling data available - all survey claims come from ruling party, creating high uncertainty about true public sentiment
Small probability (~2%) referendum is delayed/cancelled via legal challenge, which would resolve No

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.67 (67% probability Yes > 60%)**

Key reasoning:
- The ruling PNC has overwhelming institutional dominance (75/93 seats, 72/73 parliamentary vote for the amendment)
- The proposal (combining elections for cost savings) is inherently popular and easy to support
- Multiple parties (PNC, MDA, JDP) actively campaign Yes
- But the 60% threshold is non-trivial — it requires substantial margin, not just a win
- Active MDP opposition with "constitutional coup" framing, no independent polls, and economic stress create meaningful downside risk
- Monte Carlo simulation with Beta(65% center, 8% std) yields ~70% chance of clearing 60%, discounted slightly for cancellation risk
sources_consulted: [In the Maldives, referendum set for constitutional amendment on simultaneous elections](https://constitutionnet.org/news/maldives-referendum-set-constitutional-amendment-simultaneous-elections)
[(Metaculus) Will the “Yes” side receive strictly more than 60.0% of valid votes in the 2026 Maldives constitutional referendum?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42801)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if the final nationwide result for the 2026 Maldivian constitutional referendum shows the “Yes” side receiving strictly more than 60.0% of valid votes, using the Wikipedia page for the referendum at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Maldivian_constitutional_referendum. It resolves to no otherwise.
fine_print: Use the final nationwide valid-vote share cited on the Wikipedia page. If the Wikipedia page is unavailable, incomplete, or internally inconsistent by the resolution time, use the official Elections Commission of Maldives referendum result page or result release. Resolve using credible sources per https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions. Candidate Sources: Elections Commission of Maldives elections portal https://www.elections.gov.mv/en/elections
